mappings.rationaleAI: Ruskin exemplifies the practice of art criticism (B.2) with his championing of Turner and opposition to Whistler, applying criteria linking aesthetic quality to moral and social values (B.1). His insistence that Gothic craftsmanship reveals the health of society illustrates the interaction of arts with social and religious institutions (D.1, D.3).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Avant Garde's Decline and Fall in the 20th Century
mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses criteria of artistic evaluation (B.1) and critical approaches (B.2.d) through debate over whether successive avant-garde movements from Dadaism through Pop Art exhausted aesthetic possibility. The tension between aesthetic radicalism and institutional absorption by galleries and markets engages the art market (E.3) and social function of art (D.1).

Benjamin's The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction theorizes how photography and film dissolve art's traditional aura, transforming aesthetic experience (A.1, A.4.d) and the role of mechanical media in disseminating art (G.4, D.4). His critical theory linking art to political function represents a foundational critical approach applying social significance as an evaluative criterion (B.1, B.2.d).

The Poetics is the foundational text of literary and dramatic criticism, establishing evaluative criteria including catharsis (B.1), analytical critical methods (B.2.b), and enduring critical approaches to tragedy and storytelling (B.2.d). Its defence of poetry against Plato addresses meaning in art (A.2.a) and appreciation in literature and theatre (A.4.a, A.4.b).

mappings.episodeTitle: Giorgio Vasari's Lives of the Artists
mappings.rationaleAI: Vasari's Lives founded art-historical scholarship (C.1) by combining biographical method with theoretical frameworks about artistic greatness, establishing periodization and the biographical approach to criticism (B.2.b, B.2.c, B.2.d). The work's relationship to historiography illustrates how scholarship in the arts connects to other humanistic disciplines (C.2).

The Decadent movement's advocacy of art for art's sake represents a fundamental position on aesthetic criteria (B.1), rejecting moral and didactic purposes in favour of pure sensory experience (A.1, B.2.d). The moral panic provoked by Wilde's trials and debates over artistic freedom directly illustrate social control of art through censorship (D.2).

Directly engages the appreciation of art (A.3) through debate over canonical hierarchies, standards of taste, and cultural literacy. The tension between high culture and popular culture, elite gatekeeping and democratic participation, addresses diverse criteria of evaluation (B.1), critical approaches (B.2.d), and social uses of art (D.1).

Critical theory's argument that mass culture — film, radio, popular music — functions to maintain capitalist social conformity directly addresses social uses of art (D.1), critical approaches grounded in social significance (B.1, B.2.d), and the role of mechanical and electronic media in disseminating works of art (G.4).

Debates over Shakespeare's universality versus cultural specificity address how social and cultural conditioning shape the apprehension of art (A.1), problems of appreciation in literature and theatre (A.4.a, A.4.b), and whether canonical supremacy reflects genuine aesthetic merit or institutional construction (B.1). The role of education in sustaining his reputation engages arts in education (D.5).

Bohemianism as a philosophy of life lived for art directly concerns art as a vocation and the conditions of artistic work (F.2), the tension between serious artistic ambition and marginal social existence blurs professionalism and amateurism (F.3), and the broader positioning of artistic life against bourgeois convention illustrates social uses of art (D.1).

Akhmatova's Requiem, memorised line by line to evade Stalinist punishment, is a paradigmatic case of censorship and social control of art (D.2), the conditions of artistic work under political oppression (F.2), and the role of oral tradition in preserving art when written channels are suppressed (G.5).

Woolf's argument that women need financial independence and private space to produce literature directly addresses conditions of work in the arts (F.2), remuneration and rights of artists (E.4), and how gender and social structure shape artistic production (A.1, D.1). The essay itself represents a foundational feminist critical approach to literature (B.2.d).

Dürer's creation of a commercial market for prints illustrates the art market (E.3) and the role of industry and commerce in disseminating art (G.3). His training as a goldsmith's son who forged his own artistic path concerns the preparation of the artist (F.1) and art as a vocation (F.2).

Heritage sites, museums, and commemorations as selective presentations of the past directly concern the role of institutions — museums and galleries — in preserving and disseminating art and culture (G.1), and how heritage constructs national identity illustrates the social uses of art (D.1).

mappings.episodeTitle: Songs of Innocence and of Experience
mappings.rationaleAI: Blake's integration of visual art with poetry through unique relief etching raises problems of appreciation spanning literature and visual arts (A.4.a, A.4.d), while the symbolic framework of Innocence versus Experience (Lamb and Tyger) illustrates meaning, symbol, and myth in art (A.2.a, A.2.b). His illuminated printing as a mode of artistic notation connects to G.2.

The Secession's declaration of no distinction between fine and applied art represents a fundamental challenge to criteria of artistic evaluation (B.1). The programmatic break from the conservative Kunstlerhaus illustrates the interaction of arts with cultural institutions (D.1), and the movement's embrace of artistic freedom across disciplines engages professionalism and artistic identity (F.3).

Hokusai's incorporation of Western pigments and compositional techniques into traditional Japanese printmaking illustrates cross-cultural influences affecting apprehension of visual art (A.1, A.4.d). His commercial ukiyo-e woodblock printing practice exemplifies the role of industry and commerce in disseminating works of art (G.3).

Fielding's transition from satirical theatre to the novel was directly caused by government censorship of his plays, illustrating social control of art (D.2). Coleridge's praise of the plot's perfection exemplifies the practice of literary appreciation and criticism (A.4.a).

Proust's pioneering exploration of sensory experience and involuntary memory as aesthetic phenomena engages meaning in art (A.2.a) and appreciation in literature (A.4.a). His engagement with Ruskin's criticism and his influence on Bloomsbury and Woolf illustrate the transmission and application of evaluative criteria across literary cultures (B.1).

Tolstoy's renunciation of fiction in favour of moral truth directly embodies the tension between aesthetic and ethical criteria of evaluation (B.1) and the relationship between arts and moral or religious conviction (D.3). His trajectory from artistic achievement to social reform illustrates the dilemmas of art as a vocation (F.2).

Consumer culture's relationship to aesthetic experience — whether material acquisition enriches or impoverishes human life — intersects the social uses of art (D.1). The history of markets, department stores, and advertising as shapers of taste and identity engages factors affecting the economic value of art and the art market (E.1, E.3).

mappings.episodeTitle: Bruegel's The Fight Between Carnival and Lent
mappings.rationaleAI: The difficulties of interpreting Bruegel's dense visual allegory — whether he was commenting on Catholic-Protestant religious tension — directly illustrate meaning in art (A.2.a), special problems of appreciation in visual arts (A.4.d), and interpretative critical methods (B.2.b).

The Grimms' fairy tale collection as simultaneous folklore preservation, literary art, and philological scholarship illustrates scholarship in the arts (C.1) and its relation to linguistic studies and history (C.2). The tales as vehicles for symbol and myth connect to A.2.b, and the brothers' preservation of oral folk tradition connects to G.5.

The Gothic tradition's embrace of the irrational, supernatural, and sublime across literature, architecture, and visual arts illustrates symbol and myth as interpretive frameworks in the arts (A.2.b). The movement's persistence as an anti-Enlightenment aesthetic across multiple art forms represents a distinctive critical approach (B.2.d).

Beethoven's transformation of the symphony and elevation of instrumental above choral music raises special problems of appreciation in music (A.4.c). His career flourishing under aristocratic Viennese patronage illustrates systems of financing artistic activities (E.2), and composing celebrated works despite deafness exemplifies conditions of work in the arts (F.2).

The Shahnameh as national creation myth, repository of Zoroastrian themes, and vehicle for Persian linguistic preservation illustrates symbol and myth in the arts (A.2.b) and social uses of art for national identity (D.1). Its thousand-year role in oral recitation practices and manuscript illustration traditions exemplifies preservation through oral and imitative tradition (G.5, G.6).

Zen's profound shaping of Japanese aesthetic disciplines — tea ceremony, garden design, calligraphy, architecture — illustrates how religious practice and philosophy condition the apprehension of works of art (A.1) and the deep interaction between arts and religion (D.3).

The episode discusses critical approaches to Rossetti's poetry including feminist literary rediscovery and reassessment (B.2.d), and her devout Anglicanism pervading her work connects art and religion (D.3).
Dadaism challenged conventional meaning in art (A.2.a), used art as social protest against WWI (D.1), and its provocative performances pushed against social control and censorship norms (D.2).

The novella meditates on meaning in art and the relationship between beauty and artistic creation (A.2.a), and its critical reception involves diverse criteria of evaluation including aesthetic and moral significance (B.1).
The Faust myth's constant retelling reveals changing attitudes to knowledge, ambition, and damnation, functioning as symbol and myth in the arts (A.2.b).
Pessoa's heteronyms -- each with a distinctive biography and poetic voice -- raise profound questions about meaning in art, authorship, and identity (A.2.a).
The Four Quartets deeply engage with spiritual and theological questions about time, mortality, and transcendence, exemplifying the interaction of arts and religion (D.3).

The episode addresses how identity, suffering, and cultural context shape interpretation and appreciation of Kahlo's art (A.1, A.2.a on meaning in art), and her posthumous global fame reflects the social uses and reception of art (D.1).
The episode examines how Lang's films addressed mob violence, corruption, and moral ambiguity, engaging questions of social context shaping artistic meaning (A.1) and the social uses of art (D.1).

The episode engages meaning in art (A.2.a) through psychoanalytic, feminist, and postcolonial readings; Achebe's critique exemplifies diverse criteria of evaluation (B.1); the obscenity trial context touches on social control of art (D.2).
Calvino's engagement with structuralism, semiotics, and the Oulipo movement raises questions about meaning in art (A.2.a) and diverse criteria of evaluation related to technique and form (B.1).

The episode addresses meaning in art through Joyce's radical literary innovations (A.2.a) and the censorship and banning of Ulysses, culminating in the 1933 Woolsey ruling, illustrating social control of art (D.2).

The episode examines how class prejudice shaped literary gatekeeping and critical reception of Keats as a 'Cockney poet', illustrating how social conditioning affects apprehension of art (A.1) and the functions of critics (B.2.a).

mappings.episodeTitle: Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man
mappings.rationaleAI: The novel shocked readers with graphic honesty, raising questions about social conditioning in art appreciation (A.1) and social control/censorship of art (D.2).

Modernism's rejection of Victorian conventions and relationship to cubism and post-impressionism illustrate social and cultural conditioning of aesthetic experience (A.1), critical approaches to the arts (B.2.d), and the social uses of art (D.1).

The obscenity trial for offending public decency established an important precedent for literary freedom, directly illustrating social control and censorship of art (D.2), while the trial arguments engaged diverse criteria of evaluation (B.1).

The discussion of how national culture shapes gender ideals in fiction illustrates social and cultural conditioning of aesthetic experience (A.1) and critical approaches to the arts including feminist and cultural criticism (B.2.d).

The episode addresses how the Romantics' unconventional lives shaped their art and reception, connecting to the social uses of art (D.1) and social control/censorship issues (D.2) given their political radicalism.
Questions about truth and authenticity in literature connect to meaning in art (A.2.a) and diverse criteria of evaluation including moral significance and the intention of the artist (B.1).

Hazlitt's essays on literature and art constitute critical writing (B.2 covers the practice of criticism), and his philosophical engagement with meaning in art connects to interpretation of works of art (A.2.a).

Surrealist techniques of automatic writing and unexpected juxtapositions as explorations of meaning in art (A.2.a), symbol and myth rooted in the unconscious (A.2.b), and the interaction of art with Freudian psychoanalytic science (D.4).
The episode addresses critical evaluation of novels across centuries (B.1 diverse criteria of evaluation) and critical methods applied to literary works (B.2.a, B.2.d).

The concepts of catharsis, pity, and fear as interpretive frameworks for meaning in tragic art (A.2.a), and critical evaluation of whether genuine tragedy remains possible in modern democratic societies (B.1).

The cultural valorization of emotional response as a marker of virtue relates to aesthetic experience and apprehension of art (A.1), and Austen's critique of sentimental conventions as literary criticism (B.1).

Questions of biographical interpretation (Fair Youth, Dark Lady, Rival Poet) and the suppression of same-sex address engage meaning in art (A.2.a), diverse criteria of evaluation of literary quality (B.1), and critical methods of interpretation (B.2.a).

Virginia Woolf's assessment of Middlemarch as one of the few English novels written for grown-up people exemplifies critical evaluation of literary works (B.1) and critical approaches to the arts (B.2.d).

The interpretation of works of art through psychoanalytic frameworks (A.2) and psychoanalytic critical approaches to the arts (B.2.d). Harold Bloom's and Adam Phillips's approaches to reading psychoanalytic texts as literature exemplify critical methods and approaches.

The film's use of medieval iconography, morality plays, and the chess-with-Death motif involves meaning in art (A.2.a) and symbol and myth in the arts (A.2.b); its exploration of faith, doubt, and mortality through artistic form engages art and religion (D.3)

The Genesis scenes and prophets/sibyls employ rich biblical symbolism and myth (A.2.b); appreciation of the visual arts (A.4.d); the frescoes exemplify the deep interaction between arts and religion (D.3)

The debate between realism and its critics about whether faithful depiction of ordinary life constitutes art involves diverse criteria of evaluation (B.1); critical approaches to Victorian fiction from multiple perspectives (B.2.d)

Whitman's free verse broke from European metrical traditions, requiring new critical approaches to appreciate his radical innovations in poetic form (B.2.d critical approaches to the arts); his democratic poetic vision affected how audiences apprehend literary art (A.1)

Yeats's poetry pervaded by occult symbolism and cyclical theories of history exemplifies symbol and myth in the arts (A.2.b); his simultaneous work as poet and practicing magician represents a profound integration of arts and religion (D.3)
